narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 26, your profile also includes strong family support from an upper-middle income background and plans to attend law school full-time, which is the norm for most students. The admissions model evaluated your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, predicting a 78% chance that you would fail— a threshold that led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with lower predicted failure risks. This prediction wasn't based on your race or gender, but on key academic indicators from your record. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom 10% of your peers that year, which stands out well below the typical student's average decile of around 5.5 and signaled potential challenges in sustaining performance through law school. Next, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 worked somewhat in your favor compared to the average of 3.21, nudging the model slightly toward expecting bar exam success by showing solid prior achievement. However, your LSAT score of 26, far below the dataset average of 36.7, had a notable counteracting effect, reinforcing concerns about failure by highlighting a gap in standardized test readiness relative to successful bar takers. Overall, these top three elements—your lower Year 3 decile as the strongest driver toward failure, tempered by your GPA but undermined by your LSAT—overrode positives like your full-time status and family income bracket. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have higher deciles and LSATs aligning closer to averages, your profile appeared riskier for bar passage. This is tough news, especially with your background suggesting stability and dedication. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the 36-37 range, strengthening your Year 3 performance through targeted coursework or tutoring, and reapplying next cycle with updated credentials that better match successful profiles. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these gaps head-on.
